title: User Migration and the Exit Problem
thesis: Network effects and switching costs make individual exit from dominant platforms costly; interoperability, data portability, and federation can lower those costs and create space for healthier alternatives.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Network effects mean a platform's value rises with the number of users, making latecomers uncompetitive.

evidence.snippet: Mastodon describes a federated social-network model in which no single company owns the network, illustrating an alternative to winner-take-all platform structure.
OECD analysis notes that strong network effects and data-driven returns to scale can entrench dominant digital platforms and raise barriers to entry.

counterevidence.summary: Network effects can be overcome when users coordinate migration, when a niche offers superior quality, or when multi-homing reduces the cost of using several platforms at once.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Switching costs include lost contacts, content archives, and reputation.

evidence.snippet: The EU Digital Markets Act identifies gatekeeper platforms and imposes data-portability and interoperability obligations precisely because high switching costs lock users in.
OECD work highlights that switching costs tied to data lock-in, social graphs, and accumulated content reduce competitive pressure on incumbent platforms.
Klemperer's review surveys how switching costs—arising from learning, transaction, and artificial lock-in—soften competition and entrench incumbent firms.

counterevidence.summary: Many users maintain overlapping contact lists across platforms, and younger users in particular may switch services with lower perceived cost if peer groups move together.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Data portability and interoperability can reduce lock-in.

evidence.snippet: The Digital Markets Act requires gatekeepers to enable data portability and interoperability as remedies to reduce user lock-in and foster contestability.
OECD analysis argues that data portability and interoperability can increase platform contestability and user choice when implemented effectively.
Klemperer's analysis suggests that policies and design choices which reduce switching costs can restore competitive pressure and expand user choice.

counterevidence.summary: Portability alone may not change behavior if networks remain concentrated; interoperability must be technically robust, privacy-preserving, and actually used by competitors to matter.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Federated protocols like ActivityPub allow users to change providers without leaving their networks.

evidence.snippet: The W3C ActivityPub specification defines a decentralized social-networking protocol that lets users on different servers interact and follow one another.
Mastodon implements ActivityPub across independently operated servers, allowing account migration between servers while preserving follows and followers.

counterevidence.summary: Federated services face usability, discovery, moderation, and governance challenges that can limit adoption; the model shifts power to server operators rather than eliminating hierarchy.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

sources.title: OECD: Data Portability, Interoperability and Digital Platform Competition
Klemperer: Competition When Consumers Have Switching Costs

agentInstructions: Route evidentiary questions about network effects, switching costs, interoperability, and federation to the claims and source IDs in this artifact.
Preserve the cautious framing: lock-in is structural, not a personal failing of users.
Distinguish technical feasibility from adoption and usability barriers when discussing federation and interoperability.
Cite sources by sourceId and do not generalize beyond the public evidence; flag global examples that are not India-specific.
When citing the EU Digital Markets Act or OECD analysis, describe them as policy and analytical sources, not as Indian law or empirical Indian findings.
The article includes a Mermaid diagram illustrating the network-effect lock-in loop and the interoperability/federation escape path.

sectionOutline.title: Why Network Effects Feel Like a Lock
Federation and the Return of Choice
